flashcards.q: What is sound?
flashcards.a: Sound is a form of energy produced by vibrating objects that creates the sensation of hearing in our ears.

flashcards.q: What is vibration?
flashcards.a: Vibration is a rapid to-and-fro (back-and-forth) motion of an object about its mean position. All sound-producing objects vibrate.

flashcards.q: Can sound travel through vacuum? Why?
flashcards.a: No. Sound is a mechanical wave and requires a material medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to propagate. In vacuum, there are no particles to carry the disturbance.

flashcards.q: What is a mechanical wave?
flashcards.a: A wave that requires a material medium to propagate. Sound is a mechanical wave.

flashcards.q: What is compression in a sound wave?
flashcards.a: A region of high pressure and high density where medium particles are crowded together, formed when the vibrating source moves forward.

flashcards.q: What is rarefaction in a sound wave?
flashcards.a: A region of low pressure and low density where medium particles are spread apart, formed when the vibrating source moves backward.

flashcards.q: What is a longitudinal wave?
flashcards.a: A wave in which the particles of the medium oscillate parallel to the direction of propagation. Sound is a longitudinal wave.

flashcards.q: What is a transverse wave? Give one example.
flashcards.a: A wave in which particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of propagation. Example: ripples on a water surface.

flashcards.q: Is sound a longitudinal or transverse wave?
flashcards.a: Longitudinal — particles vibrate parallel to the direction of sound propagation.

flashcards.q: What travels in a sound wave — the medium particles or the disturbance?
flashcards.a: Only the disturbance (energy) travels. The medium particles oscillate about their mean positions but do not travel forward.

flashcards.q: Define wavelength (λ). What is its SI unit?
flashcards.a: The distance between two consecutive compressions or two consecutive rarefactions. SI unit: metre (m).

flashcards.q: Define frequency (ν). What is its SI unit?
flashcards.a: The number of complete oscillations per unit time. SI unit: hertz (Hz). 1 Hz = 1 oscillation per second.

flashcards.q: Define time period (T). What is its SI unit?
flashcards.a: The time taken for one complete oscillation of the wave. SI unit: second (s).

flashcards.q: State the relationship between frequency and time period.
flashcards.a: ν = 1/T  (Frequency and time period are reciprocals of each other).

flashcards.q: Define amplitude of a sound wave.
flashcards.a: The magnitude of the maximum displacement of medium particles from their mean position. Larger amplitude → louder sound.

flashcards.q: Write the wave equation connecting speed, wavelength, and frequency.
flashcards.a: v = λ × ν  (Speed = Wavelength × Frequency)

flashcards.q: Which wave property determines the pitch of sound?
flashcards.a: Frequency — higher frequency → higher pitch (shriller sound).

flashcards.q: Which wave property determines the loudness of sound?
flashcards.a: Amplitude — larger amplitude → louder sound.

flashcards.q: What is pitch?
flashcards.a: Pitch is how the brain interprets the frequency of a sound. Higher frequency → higher pitch; lower frequency → lower pitch.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between loudness and intensity?
flashcards.a: Intensity is an objective physical quantity (sound energy per second per unit area). Loudness is a subjective physiological response of the ear to sound.

flashcards.q: What is quality (timbre) of sound?
flashcards.a: The characteristic that allows us to distinguish between two sounds of the same pitch and loudness. Determined by the waveform (mixture of frequencies).

flashcards.q: What is a tone? What is a note?
flashcards.a: Tone: a sound of a single frequency. Note: a sound produced by a mixture of several frequencies; more pleasant to hear.

flashcards.q: How does the speed of sound vary with the state of the medium?
flashcards.a: Speed is greatest in solids, less in liquids, and least in gases at the same temperature.

flashcards.q: How does the speed of sound vary with temperature?
flashcards.a: Speed of sound increases as the temperature of the medium increases.

flashcards.q: What is the speed of sound in air at 22°C (approximately)?
flashcards.a: Approximately 344 m s⁻¹.

flashcards.q: State the laws of reflection of sound.
flashcards.a: 1. Angle of incidence = Angle of reflection. 2. The incident sound, reflected sound, and normal to the surface at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.

flashcards.q: What is an echo?
flashcards.a: An echo is the reflected sound that is heard after the original sound.

flashcards.q: What is the minimum time gap required between the original sound and echo for the echo to be distinct?
flashcards.a: At least 0.1 s — because the brain retains the sensation of sound for 0.1 s.

flashcards.q: What is the minimum distance of the reflecting surface for a distinct echo? (at 22°C)
flashcards.a: 17.2 m — derived from (344 m/s × 0.1 s) / 2 = 17.2 m.

flashcards.q: Write the formula to calculate the distance to a reflecting surface using echo.
flashcards.a: Distance = (v × t) / 2, where v = speed of sound and t = time for echo to return.

flashcards.q: What is reverberation?
flashcards.a: The persistence of sound in an enclosed space caused by repeated reflections from walls, ceiling, and floor.

flashcards.q: Name two materials used to reduce reverberation in an auditorium.
flashcards.a: Compressed fibreboard and rough plaster (also: draperies, acoustic tiles, carpets).

flashcards.q: Why are the ceilings of concert halls curved?
flashcards.a: To reflect sound uniformly to all corners of the hall so that every audience member hears clearly.

flashcards.q: Name one use of a stethoscope related to sound reflection.
flashcards.a: It uses multiple reflections inside its tubes to guide the faint sounds of heartbeat or breathing from the patient's body to the doctor's ears.

flashcards.q: What is the audible range of the average human ear?
flashcards.a: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz).

flashcards.q: What is infrasound? Give one example of an animal that produces it.
flashcards.a: Sound with frequency below 20 Hz, inaudible to humans. Example: rhinoceroses communicate using infrasound of about 5 Hz; also produced by whales and elephants.

flashcards.q: What is ultrasound? Give one example of an animal that uses it.
flashcards.a: Sound with frequency above 20,000 Hz (20 kHz), inaudible to humans. Example: bats use ultrasound for echolocation.

flashcards.q: Why do bats use ultrasound for echolocation and not audible sound?
flashcards.a: Ultrasound has a very short wavelength and reflects off small objects like insects. Audible sound has longer wavelengths and diffracts around small objects, giving no useful echo.

flashcards.q: What is SONAR? What does the acronym stand for?
flashcards.a: Sound Navigation And Ranging. A device that uses ultrasonic pulses to detect and measure the distance of underwater objects.

flashcards.q: Write the formula used in SONAR to find the distance of an underwater object.
flashcards.a: Distance = (v × t) / 2, where v = speed of ultrasound in water and t = time for the echo to return.

flashcards.q: How is ultrasound used to detect flaws in metal blocks?
flashcards.a: Ultrasonic waves are passed through the metal block. If a crack or flaw is present, ultrasound reflects back from it — detected by a detector — indicating the defect's location.

flashcards.q: Why can't ordinary sound detect small cracks in metal?
flashcards.a: Ordinary sound has longer wavelengths and diffracts (bends) around small defects, reaching the detector without indicating the flaw. Ultrasound's short wavelength reflects off small defects.

flashcards.q: What is echocardiography?
flashcards.a: A medical technique in which ultrasonic waves are reflected from parts of the heart to form an image, used to diagnose heart conditions.

flashcards.q: What is ultrasonography?
flashcards.a: A medical imaging technique using ultrasonic waves reflected from body tissues to generate images of internal organs like the liver, kidney, uterus, and gall bladder.

flashcards.q: True or False: The speed of sound is the same for all frequencies in a given medium.
flashcards.a: True. Speed of sound remains almost the same for all frequencies in a given medium under the same physical conditions.

flashcards.q: If the frequency of a sound doubles, what happens to its wavelength if speed remains constant?
flashcards.a: Wavelength halves. Since v = λν and v is constant, λ = v/ν — if ν doubles, λ halves.
